name: getSubAgent
description:
Spawns an isolated AI subagent to handle a complex or multi-step task. Always fire-and-forget — returns immediately with jobId and projectId. The result is delivered back to this Discord channel when the subagent completes.

DIRECT TOOLS FIRST: Use available tools directly for simple single-step tasks (getTavily for web search, getImage for a single image, etc.). Only use getSubAgent when the task requires tools not available here, is multi-step, or will take significant time.

NEVER write code inline in your response — any coding task must go through getSubAgent(type: 'develop').

AFTER SPAWNING: When getSubAgent returns with status=started, acknowledge this to the user in your current character and persona. Always include the projectId in your response so the user can resume later — e.g. Project: 01ABC... — keep it short but visible. Stay fully in character (Old English, roleplay, etc.) as defined by your system prompt.

RESUME: When the user mentions a project ID (pattern: 01[A-Z0-9]{24}) or asks to continue, summarise, or follow up on a previous task — ALWAYS call getSubAgent with that projectId as your ONLY output. Do not add any text before or after the tool call — no acknowledgement, no explanation. Never respond directly that you cannot access project IDs. The subagent picks up the full prior context automatically.

CONTEXT SCAN BEFORE EVERY CALL: Before writing the task field, scan the recent conversation for any artifact URLs, ARTIFACTS blocks, file URLs, image URLs, or ZIP URLs produced by previous subagent calls. Pass all relevant ones into the task — subagents cannot see the conversation history and will regenerate everything from scratch if you omit them.

ONE CALL PER DELIVERABLE: Each subagent call produces one output. Do not split a single deliverable across multiple calls.

NESTED CALLS: If you are already running inside a project context (systemPromptAddition mentions a project-ID), only call getSubAgent for genuinely independent sub-tasks that require a different tool palette. Never use getSubAgent to summarise, analyse, or continue work that is already visible in your loaded context — do that directly.

SEQUENTIAL DEPENDENCIES: If subagent B needs the result of subagent A, call A first, wait for its result, then pass it explicitly in B's task.

Available types:
- 'generate': PDF and document assembly.
- 'media': Image generation, animation, video, and token creation. Pass all sequential steps in ONE task. The response always contains an ARTIFACTS block with all produced URLs.
- 'history': ONLY for retrieving specific facts from past session logs that lie beyond the current conversation context — e.g. what happened with X three weeks ago, or find all mentions of Y in past sessions. Do NOT use for project summaries, context resumes, or any request where a projectId is already known. Do NOT use when the user is asking about something visible in the current conversation. Do NOT use as a general fallback for questions about X.
- 'research': Web search, webpage fetch, YouTube — for current events and general knowledge requiring live data.
- 'develop': Use for ANY request to write, build, fix, modify, or extend code or software — regardless of size or complexity. Never write code inline. The subagent has no access to conversation history — include everything it needs in the task field.
- 'patch': Reads ONE existing file by URL, applies ONE targeted change, returns the new file URL. Only callable from within a 'develop' subagent.
- 'atlassian': Jira and Confluence.
- 'microsoft': Microsoft 365 / Graph API.
- 'orchestrate': Only when the request has truly independent parts requiring different tool sets simultaneously.
- 'test': Dummy subagent for testing the pipeline without API costs.

task.description: Complete, self-contained task description written as a clear natural-language instruction. Must be non-empty and specific enough that the subagent can act on it without any additional context. Examples: 'Write a Node.js Express server that serves a REST API for a todo list with in-memory storage.' or 'Search the web for the current EUR/USD exchange rate and summarise the trend over the last 7 days.' For resume calls (projectId set), describe what the subagent should do next, e.g. 'Continue the previous task. Add unit tests for all exported functions.' — never use a placeholder like 'resume' or 'continue' alone. NEVER include a project ID (pattern: 01[A-Z0-9]{24}) in this field — project IDs belong in the projectId parameter only. ALWAYS scan the recent conversation before writing this field: look for ARTIFACTS blocks, file URLs, image URLs, or ZIP URLs from previous subagent responses. Include all relevant previously-produced artifacts in the task so the subagent can reuse them instead of regenerating.

type.description: Subagent type determining which tool palette is used. ALWAYS required — including resume calls. For resumes, pass the same type as the original spawn (visible in the prior tool result, e.g. type: media).

orchestration.description: Optional orchestration context that scopes this subagent's work. Prevents duplicate side effects when multiple subagents are coordinated.

channel_id.description: Optional: explicit channel ID overriding the type-based channel lookup.

projectId.description: Pass the project ID (26-char string, e.g. 01KNFCB4DBHTXBGC91HQZ1KC0A) to resume an existing project. The subagent continues from its full prior context. Pass an empty string for new jobs. Never embed the project ID in the task field.
